text:
[SPEARHEAD Connection - Introduction]

Tejas Tubular proprietary premium workstring connection. Double-shoulder design with internal torque shoulder, inertia-welded tool joints. Highest torque workstring connection available.

[SPEARHEAD Connection - Key Specifications]

### 2-7/8" 7.90ppf P-110
- Tool Joint OD: 3.525"
- Special Clearance (SC) OD: 3.312"
- Connection ID: 2.265"
- Min Yield Strength (TJ): 125 ksi (Tejas heat-treated P-110 is typically 125 ksi)
- Rotating Torque: 15,700 ft-lbs (Aug 2025 CDS version)
- Source: Al Aung email, Aug 4 2025; Dimitris CDS updates, Mar 4 2026

### 2-3/8" 5.95ppf P-110
- Tool Joint OD: 3.000"
- Special Clearance (SC) OD: 2.782"
- Connection ID: (same thread family)
- Source: Dimitris Katsareas, Updated CDSs email, Mar 4 2026

### Make-Up Torques (2-7/8" 7.90# P-110)
- Minimum MUT: 2,000 ft-lbs
- Optimum MUT: 4,250 ft-lbs
- Maximum MUT: 6,500 ft-lbs
- Source: John Champagne / Dimitris, CP Energy Spearhead Specs thread, Apr 1 2026

Note: Spearhead MUT values appear lower than competitors (FSS-265 max 7,200; BenHT6 max 7,000) because Tejas uses a different torque philosophy than drill pipe practice. Tejas publishes Rotating/Operating and Yield Torques in addition to MUT, which competitors do not.

[SPEARHEAD Connection - Design Features]

### Double-Shoulder Design
- Has both external and internal torque shoulders
- Internal torque shoulder (pin nose to box shoulder) provides torque capacity independent of OD
- "Torque is from internal shoulder and independent of OD for TTWS and Spearhead connection" -- Al Aung, Jan 30 2025
- This is a key advantage: torque capacity is maintained even as TJ OD wears down

### Inertia-Welded Tool Joints
- Reduced ovality and eccentricity vs upset-end connections
- Makes pin nose to box shoulder contact a true full-face contact
- Makes torque calculations more accurate
- This benefit is more significant for Spearhead (with ID torque shoulder) than for TTS6-Black (which has no ID torque shoulder)
- Source: Dimitris Katsareas, "Inertia-welded TJs vs. Upset-end" email, Feb 19 2026

### Wear Advantage Over PH6

#### 2-7/8" Size
- Spearhead: 6.43% OD-to-SC range (3.525" to 3.312")
- PH6: 3.80% OD-to-SC range (3.438" to 3.312")
- **Spearhead has 69% more remaining wear life between standard OD and SC compared to PH6**

#### 2-3/8" Size
- Spearhead: 7.84% OD-to-SC range (3.000" to 2.782")
- PH6: 4.46% OD-to-SC range (2.906" to 2.782")
- **Spearhead has 75% more remaining wear life compared to PH6**

Source: Alex Champagne / Dimitris Katsareas, "Additional Wear Spearhead vs PH6 Question" thread, Mar 2026

### Critical Wear Limit
**If joints wear below Special Clearance OD, re-tooling via inertia welding is NO LONGER POSSIBLE. The joint is effectively scrapped.**
Source: Dimitris Katsareas, Mar 10 2026

[SPEARHEAD Connection - Torque Technical Details]

### Torque Calculation Method
- Yield Torque: calculated from modified API RP 7G "screwjack" formula, adapted for double shoulder connections
- Rotating/Operating Torque: calculated from Yield Torque using Safety Factor (SF = 80% for Tejas)
- Tested and proven accurate per Tejas torque-to-yield tests
- Source: Dimitris, "Technical Information Requested from Superior" thread, Mar 27 2026

### Torque vs Competitors
Hydril PH6 and others (VAM, NORIS, TPS, Workstrings Intl) only publish min/opt/max Make-up Torques. They do NOT publish Rotating/Operating or Yield Torques.

Companies that DO publish Rotating and Yield Torques: TEJAS, BENOIT, HUNTING, PATTERSON RENTAL, PATTERSON SERVICES, QUAIL, CASEY, EAGLE, WOOLLEY.

Source: Dimitris Katsareas, Mar 27 2026

### Torque-Turn Running
- Dimitris does not believe Tejas has ever run Spearhead without torque-turn monitoring
- But does not see a problem running without it
- Must make up to at least min MUT to engage torque shoulders
- Can base on hydraulic pressure gauge reading on tongs
- Source: Dimitris, "CP Energy Updates" email, Apr 7 2026

### Asterisked Torque Figures
Torque figures with asterisk on CDS can be downrated based on rig equipment capability (tongs, swivel, rotary table). Meaning: torque as high as you can without damaging the tube.
Source: Dimitris, "Issues when achieving high torques with Spearhead" email, Jan 23 2026

[SPEARHEAD Connection - Thread Compound]

- Standard: Best-o-Life (BOL) 2000
- Alternative: JetLube ThreadSeal (lead/zinc free) is acceptable
- Any thread compound works as long as friction factor (FF) is accounted for
- BOL 2000 has FF = 1.0, so listed CDS torques apply directly
- Different compounds may require ramping torque values up or down based on their FF
- Source: Dimitris, "BOL 2000 Alternative" email, Mar 30 2026

[SPEARHEAD Connection - Identification]

- No permanent identification markings applied beyond stencil (stencil doesn't survive well runs)
- One licensee up north hard-stamped a single letter on box upset of each recut joint
- Identification left to discretion of machine shop
- Source: Dimitris, "Spearhead Information for SES" email, Mar 30 2026

[SPEARHEAD Connection - Thread Protectors]

- TEJAS does NOT license thread protector production for Spearhead
- Mold-injection requires connection drawings, which Tejas does not share
- TEJAS keeps Spearhead thread protectors always in stock for any volume
- Contact: Frank Martinez at Tejas manages thread protector production
- Source: Dimitris, "Regarding Ronnie's request about licensing SPEARHEAD thread protectors" email, Apr 4 2026

[SPEARHEAD Connection - Accessories]

### Float Valve Service Connection (2-7/8" SPH with 2F-3R)
- Designed for 2F-3R double float valve
- Service connection OD: 3.625"
- Service connection ID: 2.438" (float bore ID)
- Uses same thread form as SPH
- Torque capacity higher than standard 2-7/8 SPH
- Uses single start SPH thread -- can be recut using same SPH threading insert
- Cannot use standard 2-7/8 SPH bore (2.265" ID) because 2F-3R float has 2.406" OD, which would eliminate ID torque shoulder
- Target OD around 3.75" for casing clearance (tubing with HB already ~3.75")
- Source: Al Aung, "Spearhead Accessories Quote" thread, Jan-Feb 2026

### Pup Joints and Accessories
- All accessories including pup joints must use 135 KSI min yield mechanical tube or bar stock material
- Profiled pup joint tube: 135 KSI (meets 125 KSI min requirement)
- Accessories don't carry a "grade" -- only 135 KSI min yield to match TJ connection
- Source: Al Aung, "2.875 7.90# Spearhead TT-110 CDS" email, Aug 4 2025

### Test Plugs
- Can be made with 1/2" NPT, 6" long
- One pin, one box -- to test TIW valves
- Source: Al Aung, "Test Cap for Spearhead" email, Mar 16 2026

[SPEARHEAD Connection - Rig Running Procedure]

- TEJAS has a formal rig running procedure document for SPEARHEAD (Rev00, Jan 8 2025)
- Separate from the standard TEJAS rig running procedure
- Source: Dimitris, "Issues when achieving high torques with Spearhead" email, Jan 23 2026

[SPEARHEAD Connection - Combined Load Charts]

- Axial + Torque safe operation charts available for SPEARHEAD 2.875" 7.90ppf P-110 with 126k YS TJs
- Source: Dimitris, "Technical Information Requested from Superior" thread, Mar 27 2026

[SPEARHEAD Connection - Recuts]

See [[Recuts]] for comprehensive recut information including Spearhead-specific procedures.

[Spearhead Connection Design - Compiled Truth]

### Torque Mechanics (Fundamental Design)

- Spearhead torque is generated from the **internal shoulder** (pin nose to box shoulder)
- External shoulder contribution is **marginal**
- This is the **opposite** of PH6, where torque comes from the external shoulder
- Tool joint OD wear does NOT affect torque performance of Spearhead, whereas it will "cripple PH6"
- OD wear DOES affect **tensile efficiency** of the connection
- Torque is from internal shoulder and independent of OD for both TTWS and Spearhead



### Connection OD: 3.525" vs 3.438"

- Both 3.525" and 3.438" OD tool joints produce **identical torque values** for Spearhead (torque comes from internal shoulder, not external)
- 3.525" OD provides **more wear life** before reaching special clearance
- 3.438" OD is the **same as standard PH6** coupling OD



### Wear Life Advantage vs PH6

**2-7/8":**
- Spearhead OD 3.525" to SC 3.312" = 6.43% difference
- PH6 OD 3.438" to SC 3.312" = 3.80% difference
- **Spearhead has 69% more remaining wear life (OD to SC) compared to PH6**

**2-3/8":**
- Spearhead OD 3.000" to SC 2.782" = 7.84% difference
- PH6 OD 2.906" to SC 2.782" = 4.46% difference
- **Spearhead has 75% more remaining wear life compared to PH6**

Correct terminology: "remaining upset OD" or "remaining wear life" (similar to "remaining body wall" for tube body). Do not call it "wear area."

**Critical:** If joints wear below the special clearance, re-tooling via inertia welding is no longer possible. The whole joint is effectively scrapped.



### Inertia-Welded TJs vs Upset-End

Inertia-welded tool joints (used by Tejas) have reduced ovality and eccentricity issues compared to upset-ends (used by Benoit and PH6 lookalikes). This results in true full-face contact at the internal torque shoulder, making torque calculations accurate.

- This advantage is **more important for Spearhead** than TTS6-Black
- TTS6-Black (PH6 look-alike) has no ID torque shoulder (there is a gap there)
- Spearhead's internal shoulder benefits from the precision of inertia-welded TJs



### BEN-HT6 Technical Critique

1. Upset/TJ length has NO bearing on shoulder contact area — only diameter matters
2. Same applies for "torque distribution" and "torque stiffness" — Benoit's claims about these are incorrect
3. "Bending flexibility" is misunderstood — higher bending stiffness is actually BENEFICIAL in workover jobs (more weight on bit, less wasted to friction)
4. **Contradiction in BEN-HT6 marketing:** Cannot claim "increased shoulder contact area" AND "maintaining compatibility with BTS-6/PH6" — to be compatible, you must reduce or maintain shoulder area, not increase it

[Spearhead Connection Design - Compiled Truth]

### High Torque & Rig Running Procedure

- The asterisk on CDS torque values means: torque can be downrated based on rig equipment capability
- Practical guidance: torque as high as you can without damaging the tube
- Tejas merged John's field suggestions with their RRP (Rig Running Procedure) into Rev 01
- In practice many rigs grip tube body or fade-away instead of TJ only



### Thread Compound / Dope

- Tejas standardized on Best-o-life 2000 (BOL2000) — lead-free, but expensive and uncommon at rigs
- CDS torque values calculated at **Friction Factor 1.0** (Friction Coefficient = 0.08)
- All Tejas test torques were measured using BOL2000
- "Approved" changed to "Recommended" with a common 1.0 FF dope option added
- Specific torque values removed from running procedure text — reference CDS only



### Make-Up Torque Philosophy

MUT can equal max rotating torque (same as drill pipe approach), so connection doesn't come apart while rotating. Tejas moving away from the term "optimum MUT" and replacing maximum MUT with phrasing like "can be as high as Rotating Torque."



### Used Tubing Recut Options

- 4 alternative connection CDSs available for recut used WTS-6 tubing: TTXS, TTNY, TTUS, TTUS-HT
- **Caution:** CDS yield/torque values are based on 125 ksi YS material (90% of Tejas P-110)
- For used tubing from other manufacturers: must verify material grade
- If material is standard P-110 (110 ksi min) not Tejas-grade: multiply yield/torque values by **0.88 factor** (110/125)



### TTS6-Black Recut / Rework

- No dedicated recut/rework procedure document exists for TTS6-Black (unlike Spearhead which has one)
- Inspection procedures for TTS6-Black are on file at Tejas
- Alex Zuniga may have additional documentation



### TJ Wear vs Load Capacity (TTS6)

- Analysis exists showing load capacity degradation with wear for TTS6
- Being complemented with pull capacity, more wear increments, and torque distribution
- Analysis covers both tube body and upset OD
- TTWS removed from the analysis



### Two-Step Connection Design

- Comprehensive report from Dimitris answering many questions about two-step connection design (separate attachment)



### Key People at Tejas

- **Dimitris Katsareas** — R&D Director, connection design authority, all technical decisions
- **Al Aung** — Engineer, confirms technical calculations, connection mechanics
- **Alex Zuniga** — Manufacturing/procedures, thread compound decisions, inherited from Terry Dobson
- **Max Jr. Tejeda** — Copied on strategic discussions
- **Terry Dobson** — Previous procedure author (predecessor to Zuniga)

---

[Spearhead 2-7/8" 7.90# P-110 - Compiled Truth]

### Notes

- Double-Shoulder = Ultra-High Torque, Deep-Stabbing = Self-Aligning, DoubleStart = Fast-Makeup, Hook-Thread = No-Jumpouts
- Hardband(s) on box or/and pin end = Xtra-Long Wear-Life
- Torque calculations assume Friction Factor = 1.0 (Friction Coefficient = 0.08)
- Special Clearance OD torques and load capacities must be recalculated
- Material is EMI/SEA inspected to 5% notch, over OD/ID, long/trans and wall thickness
- Make-up Torque can be as high as Max Rotate/Operate Torque if tongs do not damage pipe

### Notes

---

[Torque Reference - Torque Nomenclature (Tejas Definitions)]

### Make-Up Torque (MUT)
- **Minimum MUT**: Lowest acceptable torque to properly engage all torque shoulders and create a seal. Traditionally set above the high shoulder torque. (Note: SM reported that 2,000 ft-lbs leaked on TTWS, so Spearhead min was set conservatively.)
- **Optimum MUT**: Target make-up torque for field operations
- **Maximum MUT**: Highest acceptable MUT without risking connection damage

### Rotating / Operating Torque
- The maximum torque the connection can sustain during drilling/workover operations
- Calculated from Yield Torque using a Safety Factor
- Tejas SF = 80% (i.e., Operating Torque = 80% of Yield Torque)
- Benoit also uses SF = 80%
- Others may differ

### Yield Torque
- Torsional capacity at which the connection begins to yield
- Calculated using modified API RP 7G "screwjack" formula, adapted for double shoulder connections
- Originally developed for drill pipe, modified by Tejas for workstring application
- Proven accurate by Tejas torque-to-yield testing

Source: Dimitris Katsareas, multiple emails including "Technical Information Requested from Superior" thread (Mar 27 2026) and Torque Nomenclature document (Apr 1 2026)

[Torque Reference - MUT Comparison Table (2-7/8" 7.90# P-110)]

| Connection | Min MUT | Opt MUT | Max MUT |
|-----------|---------|---------|---------|
| Spearhead | 2,000 | 4,250 | 6,500 |
| BEN-HT6 | 3,900 | 5,450 | 7,000 |
| FSS-265 | -- | 6,200 (rec) | 7,200 |

Note: Spearhead's lower MUT numbers are because Tejas uses different torque philosophy than drill pipe practice. FSS uses 70% yield for max MUT, 60% for opt. Tejas does not follow this practice.

Source: John Champagne / Dimitris, CP Energy Spearhead Specs thread, Mar-Apr 2026

[Torque Reference - Friction Factor and Thread Compound]

Thread compound friction factor (FF) directly affects all torque values on the CDS:
- **FF = 1.0**: Listed CDS torques apply as-is (this is the BOL 2000 baseline)
- **FF > 1.0**: Ramp down torque values
- **FF < 1.0**: Ramp up torque values

Standard compound: Best-o-Life (BOL) 2000 (FF = 1.0)
Acceptable alternative: JetLube ThreadSeal (lead/zinc free) -- also FF = 1.0

"Any thread compound will do, as long as the friction factor is taken into consideration."
Source: Dimitris Katsareas, "BOL 2000 Alternative" email, Mar 30 2026

[Torque Reference - P-110 Material Grade Considerations]

Tejas heat-treated P-110 is typically 125 ksi YS (90% of the time). All CDS torque values are calculated on this basis.

**For used tubing from other manufacturers:**
If the material is standard P-110 (110 ksi minimum), apply correction factor:
**110 / 125 = 0.88 (88%)**
to all Yield and Rotating/Operating torque values.

This is critical for workover applications where torque matters.

Source: Dimitris, Apr 10 2026

[Torque Reference - Torque Behavior Under Wear]

As tool joint OD wears down:
- Torque capacity decreases (see Reduced Torque Capacities table, Mar 10 2026)
- CDS numbers are rounded to nearest 50; calculation table has exact values
- Below Special Clearance OD, joint cannot be re-tooled and must be scrapped
- Rental companies routinely run PH6 past SC -- but this is risky

Source: Dimitris, "Additional Wear Spearhead vs PH6 Question" thread, Mar 2026

[Torque Reference - Asterisked Torque Values on CDS]

Torque figures marked with asterisks can be downrated based on rig equipment limitations:
- Rig tongs capability
- Rig swivel capability
- Rig rotary table capability

Guidance: "Torque it as high as you can, without damaging the tube."

Source: Dimitris, Jan 23 2026

[Torque Reference - Combined Load (Axial + Torque)]

Safe operation charts are available showing combined axial load + torque envelopes:
- SPEARHEAD 2.875" 7.90ppf P-110 with 126k YS TJs
- TTS6-Black 2.875 7.90ppf (conYS126, SF80, twt100, tgradeP100)

These show the interaction between tension/compression and torque capacity -- critical for completion engineering evaluation.

Source: Dimitris, "Technical Information Requested from Superior" thread, Mar 27 2026

[Torque Reference - Why Tejas Publishes More Torque Data]

Most PH6 manufacturers only publish MUT values. Tejas (and select others) publish full torque data because:
1. They use the modified API RP 7G formula to calculate actual Yield Torque
2. They apply a known Safety Factor to derive Operating Torque
3. They have validated these calculations with physical torque-to-yield testing
4. This gives operators and completion engineers much more useful data for well design

This is a key selling point when talking to technical buyers like Superior Energy.
section: Why Tejas Publishes More Torque Data

text:
[Materials and Specifications - Material Grades]

### Tejas P-110
- Tejas heat-treated P-110 achieves 125 ksi YS approximately 90% of the time
- This is significantly above the API minimum of 110 ksi
- All CDS torque values are calculated on 125 ksi basis
- Source: Dimitris Katsareas, Apr 10 2026

### TT-110 vs TT-125
- TT-110: Standard tube grade designation
- TT-125: Higher grade tube with 125 ksi min yield, paired with 135 ksi min TJ
- CDS should state TT-125 when 135 ksi TJs are specified
- Source: Al Aung / John Champagne, "2.875 7.90# Spearhead TT-110 CDS" thread, Aug 2025

### Accessory Materials
- All accessories (pup joints, nipples, crossovers) must use 135 KSI min yield material
- Can be mechanical tube or bar stock
- Profiled pup joint tube: 135 KSI (meets 125 KSI min requirement)
- Accessories don't carry an API "grade" -- only 135 KSI min yield spec
- Source: Al Aung, Aug 4 2025

[Materials and Specifications - Common Connection Dimensions]

### Internal Diameters (Connection ID)
All three Tejas workstring connections share the same ID:
- 2-7/8 SPH: **2.265"**
- 2-7/8 TTWS: **2.265"**
- 2-7/8 TTS6/PH6: **2.265"**
Source: Al Aung, Jan 21 2026

### Tool Joint ODs

| Size | Connection | TJ OD | SC OD |
|------|-----------|-------|-------|
| 2-7/8" | Spearhead | 3.525" | 3.312" |
| 2-7/8" | PH6/TTS6 | 3.438" | 3.312" |
| 2-3/8" | Spearhead | 3.000" | 2.782" |
| 2-3/8" | PH6 | 2.906" | 2.782" |

Source: Alex Champagne / Dimitris, Mar 2026

[Materials and Specifications - Tubing Length]

- Maximum length limited by temper furnace width
- Tejas targets the longest possible length (sold by foot)
- Approximately 33 ft maximum
- Source: Al Aung, Mar 27-30 2026

[Materials and Specifications - Float Valve Dimensions]

- 2F-3R float OD: 2.406"
- Standard connection bore (2.265") is too small for 2F-3R float
- 2F-3R float normally uses 3-1/2 REG connection
- SPH service connection designed with 2.438" bore ID to accommodate
- Source: Al Aung, Jan 21-23 2026

Dimitris direct: [phone removed]

[PH6 and Competitor Connections - Introduction]

Overview of the PH6 family of two-step workstring connections and their competitors.

[PH6 and Competitor Connections - Tenaris Hydril PH6]

- Industry standard two-step workstring connection
- Only publishes min/opt/max Make-up Torques (does NOT publish Rotating/Operating or Yield Torques)
- Other manufacturers that follow same practice: VAM, NORIS, TPS, Workstrings International
- Source: Dimitris Katsareas, Mar 27 2026

### 2-7/8" PH6 Key Dimensions
- Tool Joint OD: 3.438"
- Special Clearance (SC) OD: 3.312"
- Connection ID: 2.265" (same as SPH, TTWS, TTS6)

[PH6 and Competitor Connections - Benoit BEN-HT6]

- Marketed as "high torque" PH6-compatible connection
- Interchangeable with PH6/BTS-6

### BEN-HT6 Torque Analysis (from Al Aung, Oct 8 2025)
- Shows only 3.6% improvement in torsional yield over BTS-6
- BUT operating torque calculated at 90% of yield (vs 80% for BTS-6)
- This results in apparent 15.6% increase, but **only 3.6% on equal basis (both at 90%)**
- Key insight: the "higher torque" claim is partly a safety factor difference, not a true engineering improvement

### BEN-HT6 Design Contradictions (from Dimitris, Jan 29 2026)
- Cannot simultaneously have "increased shoulder contact area" AND "maintain compatibility with existing PH6 connections"
- To maintain PH6 compatibility, box counterbore ID must be equal or larger than other PH6 lookalikes -- this keeps or REDUCES external torque shoulder contact area
- Upset length has NO bearing on shoulder contact area -- only diameter matters
- Same applies for "torque distribution" and "torque stiffness" -- upset length irrelevant

### BEN-HT6 Make-Up Torques (2-7/8" 7.90#)
- Minimum: 3,900 ft-lbs
- Optimum: 5,450 ft-lbs
- Maximum: 7,000 ft-lbs
- Source: John Champagne, CP Energy Spearhead Specs thread, Mar 31 2026

[PH6 and Competitor Connections - FSS-265 (Freedom Supply Services)]

- CP Energy had boxed themselves into only having FSS in inventory (Oct 2025)
- Working Parameters: Max MUT 7,200 ft-lbs, Recommended MUT 6,200 ft-lbs
- Source: John Champagne, same thread

[PH6 and Competitor Connections - Grant Prideco Connections]

- MW (trademark) and HT (trademark) are Grant Prideco proprietary connections
- Connection information only available from Grant Prideco directly
- MW is a recent development
- HTPAC connection properties also proprietary
- Tejas cannot provide specs sheets for these
- Source: Al Aung, "Request for Pricing and Delivery Schedule" email, Feb 18 2025

[PH6 and Competitor Connections - Companies Publishing Full Torque Data]

Companies that publish max Rotating/Operating AND Yield Torques (beyond just MUT):
TEJAS, BENOIT, HUNTING, PATTERSON RENTAL, PATTERSON SERVICES, QUAIL, CASEY, EAGLE, WOOLLEY

Companies that only publish MUT values:
TENARIS/HYDRIL, VAM, NORIS, TPS, WORKSTRINGS INTERNATIONAL

Source: Dimitris Katsareas, Mar 27 2026

[PH6 and Competitor Connections - Two-Step Connection Analysis]

- Dimitris provided a report titled "Dissecting the two-step connection 1.0" (Feb 4 2026)
- Addresses questions about two-step connection design fundamentals
- Source: Dimitris, Feb 4 2026

[PH6 and Competitor Connections - RSC Connection Options (near PH6 envelope)]

- Al Aung evaluated multiple RSC connection options near PH6 OD and ID envelope (Jul 2025)
- All options fall outside the PH6 envelope
- None include a metal-to-metal seal
- Source: Al Aung, "Appropriate connection for 7.90# and 5.95# with FSS Tool Joints" email, Jul 21 2025

[PH6 and Competitor Connections - Inertia-Welded vs Upset-End Tool Joints]

A key differentiator between Tejas connections and competitors:
- Inertia-welded TJs (Tejas) have reduced ovality and eccentricity
- This matters more for connections with internal torque shoulders (Spearhead, TTWS) where true full-face contact is critical
- Less impactful for TTS6-Black/PH6 family (no ID torque shoulder)
- Higher bending stiffness from longer TJs is actually BENEFICIAL for workover -- more weight on bit, less wasted to friction
- Source: Dimitris, Feb 19 2026 and Jan 29 2026

[TenarisHydril CS Connection - Introduction]

CS (Compression Seal) is a premium integral upset tubing connection originally developed by [[Company/Hydril]] and now manufactured under the [[Company/TenarisHydril]] brand as part of the Legacy Series. It features a **dual torque shoulder** design with a **metal-to-metal three-point seal system** and **no internal recess**.

[TenarisHydril CS Connection - Quick Reference]

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Full Name | TenarisHydril CS (Compression Seal) |
| Series | Legacy Series (with [[Spec/PH6]] and PH4) |
| Type | Integral upset (hot-forged) |
| Thread Form | Bipolar, cylindrical, non-interference |
| TPI | 8 TPI (sizes <= 4-1/2"), 4 TPI (sizes >= 5") |
| Seal | Metal-to-metal, three-point seal system |
| Torque Shoulders | Dual (external + intermediate) |
| Collar | None (integral connection) |
| Internal Recess | None |
| Tension Efficiency | 100% pipe body |
| Gas-Tight | Yes (CAL IV certified) |
| H2S Service | Yes |
| Size Range | 3/4" (1.050" OD) through 7" OD |
| Grades | J-55, N-80, L-80, C-90, T-95, P-110, S-135 |
| Status | Active but de-emphasized; Legacy Series |

[TenarisHydril CS Connection - CS Designation]

"CS" stands for **Compression Seal**. The name refers to the connection's seal design where the metal-to-metal seal is energized by compression during makeup, creating gas-tight integrity without reliance on thread interference or elastomeric seals.

[TenarisHydril CS Connection - History and Development]

### Origins at Hydril Company

- [[Company/Hydril]] was founded in 1933 by Frank Roger Seaver in Torrance, California, from the Doheny Stone Drill Company. Name derived from "hydraulic drilling equipment."
- Hydril built its reputation with the first hydraulically operated blowout preventer (1930s) and became a pioneer in premium threaded connections.
- The two-step thread design that underlies both PH6 and CS dates to at least 1976 (US Patent 4,085,951).
- **US Patent 4,521,042** (filed July 5, 1983, granted June 4, 1985) by Jan W. Blackburn and Burl E. Baron at Hydril Company describes the key innovations that became the CS connection: a pipe joint with two-stepped hooked threads, dissimilar-angled positive torque shoulders, internal and external frusto-conical seals, intermediate positive-angle shoulders, and cooperating end shoulders that allow the box to trap the pin under extreme internal pressure.
- CS was likely established as a product **by the mid-1980s**, contemporaneous with PH6 (referenced as existing prior art in a 1985 European patent EP0212288A2).
- Hydril filed 200+ patents over its history covering premium connection innovations.

### Tenaris Acquisition

- **May 7, 2007:** [[Company/Tenaris]] acquired [[Company/Hydril]] for approximately $2.16 billion.
- Created the "TenarisHydril" brand, integrating premium connections into Tenaris's global OCTG portfolio.
- CS, PH6, and PH4 were grouped together as the **Legacy Series**.
- Post-acquisition, Tenaris developed the newer **Wedge Series** (Wedge 511, 533, 563, etc.) but the Legacy Series remains in production.

### Current Status (2026)

- CS is still manufactured and supplied by TenarisHydril.
- The old dedicated Legacy Series product page (tenaris.com/en/Products/PremiumConnections/LegacySeries/PH4-PH6-CS.aspx) now redirects to the main OCTG page, which only features Wedge and Blue series connections.
- However, CS blanking dimensions document (TSH-BD-40.0001, Revision 06, dated August 20, 2014) and running guidelines (GDL23376/2, October 2019, Version 03/March 2019) remain current.
- CS tubing is widely stocked by rental/service companies: Woolley Tool, Patterson Services, Quail Tools, War Horse Fishing and Rental, Energy Pipe & Equipment.
- TenarisHydril's workstrings page now only promotes the Wedge connections (533, 563, 511) — suggesting the Wedge series is positioned as the successor for new installations, while CS remains available for existing inventory and legacy applications.

[TenarisHydril CS Connection - Technical Design]

### Thread Form

- **Bipolar thread** in a **cylindrical form** (not tapered)
- Bearing surface angle: **7.5 degrees**
- Guide surface angle: **20 degrees**
- **No radial interference** between internal and external threads (non-interference design)
- Thread engagement uses negatively angled load flanks (**semi-dovetail / hooked threads**)
- Two-step thread configuration with axially separated thread sections
- Thread pitch:
  - **8 TPI** for sizes 4-1/2" and smaller
  - **4 TPI** for sizes 5" and larger

### Seal Design (Three-Point Seal System)

The CS connection employs a **three-point metal-to-metal seal system** with no elastomeric elements:

1. **External Seal / Torque Shoulder** — located at the outer end of the connection; this is also the primary torque shoulder. The 100% external pressure seal is created at this torque shoulder. The 30-degree reverse angle shoulder ensures the connection makes up to the same position every time.

2. **Intermediate Torque Shoulder** — located between the two thread steps; provides reserve torque strength and acts as a secondary seal surface. The dissimilar angles between pin and box intermediate shoulders distribute sealing load uniformly across the surface.

3. **Internal Seal** — located at the pin nose / box counterbore; provides the primary internal pressure seal. Metal-to-metal seal rated at 100% of pipe body internal pressure.

The three-point seal design means the connection seals on three separate metal-to-metal contact surfaces, providing triple redundancy for pressure containment.

### Dual Torque Shoulder Design

The defining characteristic of CS versus PH6:

- **External torque shoulder** (at the outer face) — primary torque stop
- **Intermediate torque shoulder** (between thread steps) — secondary torque stop providing reserve torque capacity

This dual-shoulder design gives CS:
- Higher over-torque capacity than single-shoulder connections
- More than 100% compression efficiency
- Enhanced fatigue resistance
- Consistent makeup position (the 30-degree reverse angle forces repeatable positioning)

### Integral Upset Construction

- Pin and box are machined on **hot-forged upsets** — this is what achieves **100% pipe body tension strength**
- The streamlined OD configuration of the upset provides:
  - Hang-up free running in multiple string completions
  - Same turned OD on both pin and box
- No coupling (collarless design) — reduces OD profile
- No internal recess — maintains full-bore ID for flow

### Corrosion Barrier (CB) Option

CS is available with an optional **Corrosion Barrier (CS-CB)** configuration, noted in patent literature as an example of "fully coatable tubing." The CB option provides additional corrosion protection for the seal areas in aggressive well environments.

[TenarisHydril CS Connection - Performance Characteristics]

### Structural Performance

- **Tensile efficiency:** 100% of pipe body (due to hot-forged upset)
- **Compression efficiency:** Greater than 100% (dual shoulder design)
- **Collapse resistance:** Optimized stress distribution for high collapse and burst
- **Internal pressure seal:** 100% of pipe body rating (metal-to-metal)
- **External pressure seal:** 100% (created at torque shoulder)

### Gas-Tight Capability

- **ISO 13679 CAL IV certified** — the highest gas-tight qualification level
- CAL IV = full gas-tight certification at 100% yield pressure using gas as the test medium
- Helium mass spectrometer and bubble testing per ISO 13679
- Suitable for high-pressure gas storage and offshore completions

### Sour Service

- **H2S service suitable** — low-stress thread profile with fixed makeup position
- Compliant with NACE MR-0175 / ISO 15156 when using appropriate material grades
- Available in sour service grades (L-80, C-90, T-95)

### Reusability

- Non-interference thread design provides excellent reusability
- Metal seals maintain integrity through multiple make-and-break cycles
- Substantial torque shoulders resist galling
- Design validated through make-and-break testing plus finite-element analysis

[TenarisHydril CS Connection - Makeup and Running Procedures]

### Pre-Running

1. Never move or handle pipe without thread protectors securely in place
2. Clean connections free of all debris and contaminants
3. Visually inspect threads and seal areas — no damage
4. Verify connections are genuine TenarisHydril manufactured
5. Verify compatibility with accessories (pup joints, crossovers, safety valves)
6. Verify material grade compatibility of all accessories

### Thread Compound Application

1. Apply a thin coating of thread compound on the **full pin end only** — threads, seals, and pin nose. Thread form should be clearly visible through the compound.
2. **Do NOT apply running compound to the box end.**
3. Clean compound from the box if received "rig ready."
4. Do not apply the manufacturer's indicated friction factor.

### Running

1. Use a stabbing guide (strongly recommended)
2. Use a weight compensator for chrome pipe
3. Use **slip type elevators** — never use drill pipe elevators for CS connections
4. Stab pipe in a smooth, controlled fashion with pipe vertical
5. Initial rotation at low RPM (5 RPM or below) to prevent cross-threading
6. If cross-threading occurs, immediately reverse, disassemble, clean, and inspect
7. Maximum spin-in speed: **15 RPM**
8. Apply power tong at low RPM (do not exceed 5 RPM) for final makeup
9. Tong jaws must always be on the **pipe body**, never on the connection OD
10. Walk chrome pipe all the way in to hand tight, then apply tong only for final makeup

### Torque Application

1. Computer makeup analysis equipment is strongly recommended (especially for chrome material)
2. There are **no defined shoulder points** for CS, however a shoulder point must be in evidence
3. Shoulder point must be below minimum makeup torque
4. Set reference torque at zero
5. Set dump valve at optimum torque to verify correct pipe body operation before first makeup
6. Set computer turns to 1 initially, then adjust for good graph depiction
7. The makeup profile should show a gradual rise through thread engagement, then a sharp spike at shoulder contact

### Pulling

1. Use automatic stabbing system or stabber to maintain vertical position
2. Use stabbing guide to centralize the pin
3. Use weight compensator for chrome pipe
4. Use slip type elevators
5. Apply backup tong jaw on pipe body — do not grip connection OD
6. Power tong at low RPM (3-5 RPM) to break connection
7. Do not exceed 15 RPM during spin out
8. Walk chrome pipe fully out after initial breakout
9. Visual inspection to classify thread condition
10. Apply clean, dry thread protectors after applying storage compound

[TenarisHydril CS Connection - CS vs PH6 vs PH4 Comparison]

| Feature | CS | PH6 | PH4 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Threads Per Inch | 8 (<=4.5"), 4 (>=5") | 6 | 4 |
| Torque Shoulders | **Dual** (external + intermediate) | Single (external) + intermediate | Single (external) + intermediate |
| Thread Type | Bipolar cylindrical, hooked | Two-step, hooked | Two-step, hooked |
| Seal Points | Three-point M2M | Three-point M2M | Three-point M2M |
| Collar | None (integral) | None (integral) | None (integral) |
| Construction | Hot-forged upset | Hot-forged upset | Hot-forged upset |
| Tensile Efficiency | 100% | 100% | 100% |
| Gas-Tight | CAL IV | CAL IV | CAL IV |
| Interchangeable | **NOT interchangeable with PH6 or PH4** | NOT interchangeable with CS or PH4 | NOT interchangeable with CS or PH6 |
| Size Range | 3/4" to 7" | Standard tubing/workstring sizes | Standard tubing/workstring sizes |

**Key Differences:**
- CS has **dual torque shoulders** with distinct external and intermediate shoulders, while PH6 and PH4 have a 30-degree reverse-angle external torque shoulder with a 90-degree intermediate shoulder
- CS uses **8 TPI** (vs 6 for PH6 and 4 for PH4 — the numbers in their names indicate TPI)
- CS extends down to **macaroni tubing sizes** (3/4") — this is its dominant niche for small-diameter applications
- **PH6** is the industry standard workstring connection; **CS** sees more use in production tubing and small-diameter/macaroni tubing
- **None of these three connections are interchangeable with each other**
section: CS vs PH6 vs PH4 Comparison

text:
[TenarisHydril CS Connection - Applications]

### Primary Applications
- **Production tubing** — gas wells, oil wells, especially where gas-tight integrity is critical
- **Macaroni tubing** — dominant connection for small-diameter tubing (3/4" to 2-1/16") used in:
  - Artificial gas lift wells
  - Velocity string installations
  - Slim-hole completions
- **Workstring applications** — drilling cement, milling, fishing, high-pressure DST
- **Washover pipe** — used as the connection form for washover pipe assemblies
- **Pup joints** — short lengths for spacing out downhole assemblies

### Well Types
- High-pressure gas wells
- H2S / sour service environments
- Offshore completions
- High-pressure gas storage wells
- Heavy-wall tubing strings
- Multiple string completions (streamlined OD allows hang-up free running)
- Directional wells (dual shoulder provides superior torque capacity)

### Market Position
- **Widely stocked** by major rental and service companies across North America
- Key stocking companies: Woolley Tool (Kilgore TX, OKC), Patterson Services, Quail Tools, War Horse Fishing, Energy Pipe & Equipment
- Third-party manufacturers produce "CS Hydril Type" or "CS equivalent" tubing (Forwell, Nova Energy, Chinese manufacturers)
- CS remains deeply entrenched in the macaroni tubing market where few premium alternatives exist

[TenarisHydril CS Connection - Running Manual Reference]

- **Document:** TenarisHydril PH6 / PH4 / CS Running Guidelines
- **IDM Code:** GDL23376/2
- **Date:** October 2019
- **Version:** 03 / March 2019
- **Blanking Dimensions Document:** TSH-BD-40.0001, Revision 06, August 20, 2014
- **Field Inspection Guidelines:** GDL31457
- **Approved Thread Compounds:** Document FTD29356

[TenarisHydril CS Connection - Equivalent / Compatible Connections]

Several third-party manufacturers produce CS-equivalent connections:
- **ZC-5** (ZC Pipe) — marketed as CS equivalent with ISO 13679 CAL IV qualification
- Various Chinese manufacturers (Forwell Energy, Nova Energy, Kunyuan) produce "Hydril CS equivalent" or "CS Type" tubing and pup joints
- These equivalents are NOT verified as interchangeable by TenarisHydril — compatibility should be confirmed before mixing in a string

[TTS6-Black Connection - Introduction]

Tejas Tubular's PH6-compatible workstring connection. Single torque shoulder design (external only, gap at ID). Interchangeable with PH6 and other two-step connections.

[TTS6-Black Connection - Key Specifications]

### 2-7/8" 7.90ppf P-110
- Connection type: Two-step, single external torque shoulder
- PH6-compatible / interchangeable with BTS-6, BEN-HT6, and other PH6 lookalikes
- No internal (ID) torque shoulder (there is a gap)
- Source: Dimitris Katsareas, multiple emails 2025-2026

[TTS6-Black Connection - Torque Details]

### Why TTS6-Black Has Higher Torque Than Tenaris Hydril PH6
- TEJAS has tested the connection to torque-to-yield levels and proven it can handle much higher torques than HYDRIL publishes
- HYDRIL only publishes min/opt/max Make-up Torques, NOT Rotating/Operating or Yield Torques
- TEJAS publishes all torque values, calculated from modified API RP 7G formula with SF = 80%
- Source: Dimitris, "Technical Information Requested from Superior" thread, Mar 27 2026

### Torque-to-Yield Tests
- TEJAS performed formal torque-to-yield tests on TTS6-Black 2.875 7.90ppf P110 (report dated Mar 28 2025)
- Tests prove torque capacity claims are accurate
- Source: Dimitris, same email thread

[TTS6-Black Connection - Unique Identification Features]

### Thread Relief Grooves (Primary Identifier)
- TEJAS has embedded thread reliefs in both big and small threads
- Appear as shallow grooves visible to the naked eye
- These are part of the drawing and CNC program
- **No other PH6 manufacturer uses these features** (to Tejas's knowledge)
- Big thread relief groove is more noticeable than the small one
- Licensees MUST include these when recutting TTS6-Black
- Source: Dimitris, "RigPal Meeting Followup Information" email, Apr 8 2026

### Engraving (Historical, Discontinued)
- Previously used rolling/knurling tool to engrave markings
- No longer done by Tejas
- Would be machined off during recuts anyway
- Could be reintroduced if required, but would need licensee buy-in
- Source: same email

### Monogram Stamping (Optional)
- Can stamp TEJAS monogram (double-T in circle) on OD of box/pin upset or upset fadeaway
- One licensee has done single-letter stamping on box upset when recutting TTS6
- Considered superfluous since thread reliefs already identify the connection
- Source: same email

[TTS6-Black Connection - Inspection Procedures]

- TTS Box Gaging Procedure (Rev 1)
- TTS Pin Gaging Procedure (Rev 1)
- Source: Dimitris, "TTS6-Black Recut / Re-work procedure" email, Mar 30 2026

[TTS6-Black Connection - Wear vs Load Capacity]

- Dimitris working on comprehensive TJ wear vs load capacity analysis for TTS6-Black
- Will include pull capacity, multiple wear increments, and torque distribution
- Similar analysis already completed for SPEARHEAD
- Source: Dimitris, "TJ wear vs. load capacity TTS6" email, Mar 30 2026

[TTS6-Black Connection - Combined Load Charts]

- Axial + Torque safe operation chart available for TTS6-Black 2.875 7.90ppf
- Parameters: conYS126, SF80, twt100, tgradeP100
- Source: Dimitris, Mar 27 2026

[Recuts Reference - Recut Connection Options for Used Tubing]

### Used WTS-6 2-7/8" 7.90ppf P-110 Recut Options (from Dimitris, Apr 10 2026)
Tejas offers the following recut connection options:
- **TTXS** -- CDS dated 4-10-2026 (2.875 7.80ppf P-110)
- **TTNY** -- CDS dated 4-10-2026 (2.875 7.80ppf P-110)
- **TTUS** -- CDS dated 4-10-2026 (2.875 7.90ppf P-110)
- **TTUS-HT** -- CDS dated 4-10-2026 (2.875 7.90ppf P-110)

**CRITICAL NOTE on material grade:** Yield and Rotating/Operating torque values in these CDSs are calculated based on 125 ksi YS material (what Tejas heat-treated P-110 is 90% of the time). For used tubing from other manufacturers, you MUST verify material grade meets Tejas requirements. If the material is standard P-110 (110 ksi), apply a correction factor of 110/125 = 0.88 (88%) to all Yield and Rotating/Operating torque values.

This is especially important for workover applications where torque is critical.

Source: Dimitris Katsareas, "Used WTS-6 2-7/8 7.90ppf P-110 tubing recut T&C connection options" email, Apr 10 2026
section: Recut Connection Options for Used Tubing

text:
[Recuts Reference - Recut Procedures by Connection]

### SPEARHEAD
- Has a dedicated Re-cut / Re-work procedure document
- Confirmed by Dimitris (Mar 30 2026) -- "like we have for SPEARHEAD"
- 2F3R SPH service connection uses single start SPH thread and can be recut using same SPH threading insert
- Source: Al Aung, Feb 3 2026

### TTS6-Black
- Inspection procedures available: TTS Box Gaging Procedure (Rev 1), TTS Pin Gaging Procedure (Rev 1)
- As of Mar 30 2026, Dimitris was unsure if a separate recut/rework procedure doc exists for TTS6-Black (may exist with Alex Zuniga)
- Source: Dimitris, "TTS6-Black Recut / Re-work procedure" email, Mar 30 2026

[Recuts Reference - Wear Limits and Recut Feasibility]

### Critical Limit: Special Clearance (SC) OD
**If tool joint OD wears below Special Clearance, re-tooling via inertia welding is NO LONGER POSSIBLE. The joint must be scrapped.**

This is the absolute wear limit -- there is no coming back from below SC.

Source: Dimitris Katsareas, Mar 10 2026

### Reduced Torque at Worn ODs
- Dimitris created a "Reduced Torque Capacities due to Tool Joint OD wear" table (Mar 10 2026)
- Numbers are exact from calculations (CDS values are rounded to nearest 50)
- Available for Spearhead; similar analysis in progress for TTS6-Black
- Source: Dimitris, "Additional Wear Spearhead vs PH6 Question" thread

### Turnkey Wear Limits
- Turnkey has their own wear limit practices (referenced by Dimitris)
- Dimitris shared what Turnkey practices regarding wear limits alongside Tejas data
- Source: Dimitris, Mar 5 2026

### Wear Terminology
- "Remaining upset OD" or "remaining wear life" are correct terms for the OD-to-SC range
- "Wear area" is incorrect -- that term describes contact area (sq inches) between tube upset and casing
- "Remaining body wall" is analogous term used for tube body
- Source: Dimitris, Mar 3 2026

[Recuts Reference - Licensee Recutting]

### Licensee Shops
- Separate licensee lists exist for TTS6/TTS6-Black and TTWS/SPEARHEAD
- Tejas Licensee Contact List (dated Oct 23 2025) available
- Ashley Zamora at Tejas manages licensees
- Source: Dimitris, Mar 27 2026

### Known Licensees / Under Review
- **Pathfinder** -- Holds license for TTS6-Black, has most of what's needed to produce/recut SPEARHEAD (as of Feb 2 2026)
- **Integrity Machine** (Odessa, W. Texas) -- Under review. Had optical comparator issue that needed resolution before audit (as of Feb 2 2026). As of Apr 7 2026, they claim to be ready but Tejas audit not yet completed.
- Source: Dimitris, "Licensee under review: INTEGRITY" emails, Feb 2 2026 and Apr 7 2026

### TTS6-Black Recut Identification
When licensees recut TTS6-Black, they MUST include the thread relief grooves (unique Tejas identifier built into CNC program). This is a licensing requirement.
Source: Dimitris, Apr 8 2026

[Recuts Reference - Recut Cost Context]

- Licensee shops charge for recuts; there is a Tejas licensing cost component passed through
- "I think you have these numbers" -- Dimitris to Alex, suggesting RigPal has pricing info
- Source: Dimitris, Mar 27 2026

[Benoit BEN-HT6 2-7/8" 7.90# P-110 (Competitor) - Compiled Truth]

### Overview

Improved version of Benoit's legacy BTS-6 connection. Designed to increase torque capacity and connection life while maintaining **compatibility with BTS-6 and existing industry two-step connections** (i.e., PH6 interchangeable). Patent pending.

### Tubular Data

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Tube OD | 2.875 in |
| Tube ID | 2.323 in |
| Tube Wall | 0.276 in |
| Drift Diameter | 2.229 in |
| Pipe Body CSA | 2.254 sq.in |
| Tension Yield | 247,900 lbs |
| Compression Yield | 247,900 lbs |
| Internal Pressure Yield | 18,480 psi |
| Collapse Pressure Yield | 19,090 psi |
| Bending Yield | 175.0 deg/100ft |
| Torsion Yield | 14,160 ft-lb |

### Connection Data

| Property | Value | Efficiency |
|----------|-------|------------|
| **Connection OD** | **3.438 in** | |
| Connection ID | 2.265 in | |
| Make-up Loss | 3.050 in | |
| Connection CSA | 2.417 sq.in | |
| String Length (1.6 S/F) | 19,620 ft | |
| Tension Yield | 265,900 lbs | 107% |
| Compression Yield | 289,600 lbs | 117% |
| Internal Pressure Yield | 18,480 psi | |
| Collapse Pressure Yield | 19,090 psi | |
| Bending Yield | 175.0 deg/100ft | |
| Torsion Yield | 11,240 ft-lb | |
| Operating Torque | 10,030 ft-lb | |

### Make-Up Torque

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Minimum Make-up Torque | 3,900 ft-lb |
| Optimum Make-up Torque | 5,450 ft-lb |
| Maximum Make-up Torque | 7,000 ft-lb |

### Spearhead vs BEN-HT6 Comparison (2-7/8" 7.90# P110)

| Property | Spearhead | BEN-HT6 | Advantage |
|----------|-----------|---------|-----------|
| Connection OD | 3.525 in | 3.438 in | BEN-HT6 (smaller profile) |
| Special Clearance OD | 3.312 in | N/A | Spearhead (offers SC option) |
| Connection ID | 2.265 in | 2.265 in | Tie |
| Tensile Efficiency | 121% | 107% | **Spearhead** |
| Compression Efficiency | 121% | 117% | **Spearhead** |
| Torque Efficiency | 108% | N/A | Spearhead |
| Max Rotate/Operate Torque | 14,500 ft-lb | 10,030 ft-lb | **Spearhead (+44%)** |
| Yield Torque | 15,300 ft-lb | 11,240 ft-lb | **Spearhead (+36%)** |
| Optimum MUT | 4,250 ft-lb | 5,450 ft-lb | Similar |
| Maximum MUT | 6,500 ft-lb | 7,000 ft-lb | Similar |
| BSR | 1.25 | N/A | Spearhead (listed) |
| PH6 Compatible | Yes | Yes (BTS-6 compatible) | Tie |

**Key selling point for Spearhead:** Significantly higher torque capacity (44% more operating torque, 36% more yield torque) and better tensile/compression efficiency. Spearhead also offers special clearance OD option.

---

[Tejas Licensee Program - Qualification Process]

Three-step process managed by Ashley Zamora at Tejas:

### Step 1: Questionnaire
- Covers machining and inspection equipment
- Documentation and certificates required
- Must be received and approved before proceeding

### Step 2: NDA and Audit
- NDA is sent and signed
- In-person audit performed by qualified TEJAS technician
- Key equipment requirement: optical comparator (for inspecting seal and torque shoulders)

### Step 3: License Agreement
- License Agreement signed by both parties
- TEJAS issues a License Certificate to the licensee

Source: Dimitris Katsareas, "Licensee under review: INTEGRITY" email, Feb 2 2026

[Tejas Licensee Program - Licensee Lists]

- Separate licensee lists maintained for:
  - TTS6 / TTS6-Black
  - TTWS / SPEARHEAD
- Tejas Licensee Contact List exists (dated Oct 23 2025)
- Source: Dimitris, Mar 27 2026

### Pathfinder
- Holds license for making TTS6-Black

### Integrity Machine (Odessa, W. Texas)

[Tejas Licensee Program - Licensee Requirements]

### TTS6-Black Recutting
- Must include thread relief grooves (unique Tejas identifier in CNC program)
- This is a mandatory licensing requirement
- Source: Dimitris, Apr 8 2026

### SPEARHEAD Thread Protectors
- TEJAS does NOT license thread protector production
- Mold-injection requires connection drawings (not shared)
- TEJAS keeps protectors in stock for any volume
- Frank Martinez manages thread protector production at Tejas
- Source: Dimitris, Apr 4 2026

- RigPal should have these numbers on file
